nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes, lagoon edges, and channel margins; typically forms aggregations 1–2 m above coral or rocky substrate at depths of 2–30 m (most commonly 5–20 m).

diet.notes: The Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse is primarily a zooplankton feeder in the wild. In captivity it generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen copepods, frozen brine shrimp, and high-quality marine pellets. Multiple small feedings per day tend to produce better results than one or two larger meals.

avoidWith: other male Cirrhilabrus in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2507x1880; sharp, well-framed terminal male showing the diagnostic blue head/side plus distinct yellow flank patch that matches the Yellow-Flanked/Blue-Sided common name.
Rickard Zerpe; 2611x1958; crisp, vibrant terminal male with blue head and orange-red body, full lateral profile against dark reef.
Rickard Zerpe; 2534x1901; sharp terminal male in a different colour expression (red back, purple spotting, green-yellow rear) for variety.

summary: A colorful and generally peaceful fairy wrasse from the Indo-West Pacific, commonly regarded as reef-safe and well-suited to established reef aquariums of at least 200 L with ample open swimming space.
careNotes: The Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus cyanopleura) is distributed across the Indo-West Pacific, from the Andaman Sea and Similan Islands through the Philippines, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Palau, and south to the Great Barrier Reef and Christmas Island, with additional records from the Ryukyu Islands and Taiwan. In the wild it tends to form aggregations 1–2 m above reef structure along lagoon margins and outer reef slopes, typically at depths of 5–20 m. Males are markedly more colorful than females — the species exhibits clear sexual dimorphism typical of the genus, and males often intensify coloration during courtship displays. Like most Cirrhilabrus wrasses, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite; females may transition to male in the absence of a dominant male. It is best kept as a single male, or as a male introduced simultaneously with one or more females; multiple males in a small system have been known to conflict. A tight-fitting lid or euro-brace is strongly advised — fairy wrasses are documented jumpers, and open aquariums carry a real risk of escape. The species is commonly considered reef-safe and has not been widely reported to nip corals or ornamental invertebrates. Note: the trade name 'Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse' is also applied by some retailers to Cirrhilabrus ryukyuensis (Ryukyu Wrasse), a distinct species from the Western Pacific with a more restricted range; buyers should verify the scientific name before purchase.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has rarely been documented. The Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning dominant females may transition to male if no male is present. In the wild, spawning involves distinct pairing behavior, with males performing elaborate color displays to court females.

additional.notes: Primary source URL (collection page) did not have a direct listing for C. cyanopleura. The LiveAquaria product page titled 'Yellow-Flanked Fairy Wrasse' resolves to Cirrhilabrus ryukyuensis, a different species — water parameters (72–78°F / 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12) and escape-risk warning from that page are referenced as supplementary context for the genus but not used as primary data for this entry. Cross-source role filled by FishBase and BlueZoo Aquatics.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning is rarely documented for this species. It is a protogynous hermaphrodite, and the dominant female in a group may transition to male if no male is present. Wild spawning involves distinct pairing behavior, with males performing elaborate courtship color displays.
